title: When Empires Can't Pay Their Soldiers: The Civilizational Decay Playbook

source.original: You Have 10 Years to Join the AI Economy | Michael Saylor

body_html:
Every great empire follows a predictable arc: it rises through discipline, scarcity, and virtue, peaks with abundance and power, then overextends through grandiose ambitions until it collapses under its own weight. Michael Saylor draws a direct line from ancient Rome to modern Western democracies, arguing that the pattern is not merely analogous — it is identical in its mechanics. The moment a civilization believes it can right every wrong and fund every entitlement, the countdown to decline begins.

The tell-tale sign of imperial overreach is brutally simple: you can no longer pay your soldiers. In the Roman context, that meant literal mutiny. In the modern UK, Saylor argues, the equivalent is a bloated public sector whose wages can no longer keep pace with inflation. This "army of voters" — teachers, doctors, civil servants — was cultivated to sustain political power, but the cost has become unsustainable. Discontent fractures the social contract from within.

Saylor coins the term political metabolic disease to describe this phenomenon. Everything — government, charity, banking, education, medicine, defense, foreign policy — simply gets too big. He compares it to biological limits in nature: there are no 100-ton land mammals because at a certain mass, a creature collapses under its own weight. The elephant is nature's ceiling. Civilizations have ceilings too, and most blow past them.

The counter-examples are illuminating. Singapore rose from poverty by implementing rational tax policy, free trade, English as a communication protocol, and rule of law. Dubai built a thriving metropolis from a waterless desert through similar principles. These are civilizations that embraced humility — not because they chose virtue abstractly, but because they had nothing and therefore couldn't afford delusion.

The uncomfortable implication is that the places universally admired today — Paris, London, New York, California — are precisely the ones most vulnerable to decay. Their wealth creates the illusion that any social program is affordable, any entitlement is justified. First it's universal healthcare, then universal income, then confiscation to fund the mandate. The pattern has repeated for millennia, and Saylor sees no evidence that modern democracies are exempt.
